runs.questionText: Set up a Proxmox home server for self-hosting
runs.answerText:
## Setting Up a Proxmox Home Server for Self-Hosting

Proxmox Virtual Environment (VE) is a free, open-source Type-1 hypervisor built on Debian Linux that lets you run multiple isolated virtual machines (VMs) and lightweight containers on a single physical machine. It combines KVM for full VMs and LXC for Linux containers, managed through a clean web interface — giving you snapshots, live migration, and proper backups without the licensing cost of VMware or Hyper-V. [2](#a1778fcc-dc77-41ea-adc9-de85aa9e800b)

### What You’ll Need

Before starting, gather the following hardware and tools:

- **A dedicated x86-64 machine** with at least 8 GB of RAM (16 GB recommended, especially if using ZFS). [1](#a1778fcc-dc77-41ea-adc9-de85aa9e800b)
- **CPU with Intel VT-x or AMD-V** virtualization extensions enabled in BIOS/UEFI. VT-d/AMD-Vi is recommended if you plan PCI passthrough. [1](#a1778fcc-dc77-41ea-adc9-de85aa9e800b)
- **One SSD or NVMe** (120 GB minimum, 250 GB+ comfortable) for the Proxmox host. [2](#a1778fcc-dc77-41ea-adc9-de85aa9e800b)
- **A second drive** (optional but recommended) for VM storage; a ZFS mirror pair works well. [2](#a1778fcc-dc77-41ea-adc9-de85aa9e800b)
- **Wired Ethernet connection** — Wi-Fi is not supported as a primary network interface. [2](#a1778fcc-dc77-41ea-adc9-de85aa9e800b)
- **USB flash drive** (8 GB+) for the installer. [2](#a1778fcc-dc77-41ea-adc9-de85aa9e800b)
- **A monitor and keyboard** for initial setup, plus a second computer on the same network to access the web UI. [2](#a1778fcc-dc77-41ea-adc9-de85aa9e800b)

For most home users, a mini PC with an Intel N100/N305 or similar draws only 10–15 watts idle and handles a serious self-hosted stack comfortably. [1](#a1778fcc-dc77-41ea-adc9-de85aa9e800b)

### Step 1: Download and Prepare the Installer

1. Download the latest Proxmox VE ISO from `proxmox.com/en/downloads`. As of mid-2026, Proxmox VE 9.1 is current and ships with Debian 13 “Trixie,” Linux kernel 6.14, QEMU 10.0, and ZFS 2.3. [2](#a1778fcc-dc77-41ea-adc9-de85aa9e800b)
2. Verify the SHA256 checksum against the official downloads page. Do not skip this — a corrupted ISO causes baffling install failures. [2](#a1778fcc-dc77-41ea-adc9-de85aa9e800b)
3. Write the ISO to a USB drive using **Rufus** (Windows), **balenaEtcher**, or `dd` on Linux/macOS. If Rufus asks about ISO vs. DD Image mode, choose **DD Image mode**. [2](#a1778fcc-dc77-41ea-adc9-de85aa9e800b)

### Step 2: Install Proxmox VE

Plug the USB into your server, boot from it, and select **Install Proxmox VE (Graphical)**.

**Key installer decisions:**

- **Target disk:** Choose your SSD/NVMe. If you have two drives, you can select both for ZFS mirroring. [3](#a1778fcc-dc77-41ea-adc9-de85aa9e800b)
- **Filesystem:**
  - **ext4 (LVM)** — default, simple, fast. Best for single-disk setups or beginners. [3](#a1778fcc-dc77-41ea-adc9-de85aa9e800b)
  - **ZFS** — provides snapshots, checksums, compression, and self-healing. Ideal if you have 16 GB+ RAM and two or more drives. Budget roughly 1 GB of RAM per terabyte of ZFS storage for the ARC cache. [3](#a1778fcc-dc77-41ea-adc9-de85aa9e800b)
  - **Btrfs** — marked “technology preview.” Skip unless you have a specific reason. [3](#a1778fcc-dc77-41ea-adc9-de85aa9e800b)
- **Network:** Set a **static IP** outside your DHCP range (e.g., `192.168.1.50/24`), gateway, and DNS. Use a fully qualified hostname like `pve.home.lan`. [2](#a1778fcc-dc77-41ea-adc9-de85aa9e800b)
- **Password:** Set a strong root password and provide a real email address for alerts. [2](#a1778fcc-dc77-41ea-adc9-de85aa9e800b)

The install takes 5–10 minutes. Remove the USB when prompted and reboot. [2](#a1778fcc-dc77-41ea-adc9-de85aa9e800b)

### Step 3: Initial Configuration (Post-Install)

After reboot, the console shows the web UI address: `https://<your-ip>:8006`. Open this from another computer on the same network. Accept the self-signed certificate warning and log in as `root`. [2](#a1778fcc-dc77-41ea-adc9-de85aa9e800b)

**Critical first steps:**

1. **Fix repositories:** By default, Proxmox points to the enterprise repo, which requires a paid subscription. You must switch to the free **No-Subscription** repository or updates will fail with a 401 Unauthorized error. [4](#a1778fcc-dc77-41ea-adc9-de85aa9e800b)
   - In the web UI: select your node → **Updates → Repositories** → disable `pve-enterprise` and `ceph-enterprise` entries → click **Add** → choose **No-Subscription**. [2](#a1778fcc-dc77-41ea-adc9-de85aa9e800b)
   - Alternatively, via shell: disable the enterprise `.sources` file by setting `Enabled: no`, then create `/etc/apt/sources.list.d/proxmox.sources` with the official `pve-no-subscription` URI for the `trixie` suite. [4](#a1778fcc-dc77-41ea-adc9-de85aa9e800b)

2. **Update the system:**
   ```bash
   apt update && apt full-upgrade -y
   reboot
   ```
   Use `full-upgrade` (not just `upgrade`) so kernel and `pve-manager` packages are not held back. [4](#a1778fcc-dc77-41ea-adc9-de85aa9e800b)

3. **Remove the subscription nag (optional):** The “No valid subscription” popup is cosmetic but annoying. You can suppress it by editing the JavaScript check in `/usr/share/javascript/proxmox-widget-toolkit/proxmoxlib.js` and restarting `pveproxy`. Note that updates may restore this file, so the popup can return after major upgrades. [6](#a1778fcc-dc77-41ea-adc9-de85aa9e800b)

### Step 4: Storage Setup

Proxmox automatically creates `local` (ISOs/templates) and `local-lvm` (VM disks) storage on a single-drive install. [6](#a1778fcc-dc77-41ea-adc9-de85aa9e800b)

If you have a second drive:
- **For ZFS:** Create a pool (e.g., `zpool create datastore /dev/nvme1n1`) and add it under **Datacenter → Storage → Add → ZFS**. [6](#a1778fcc-dc77-41ea-adc9-de85aa9e800b)
- **For external backups:** Mount a USB drive to `/mnt/backup`, add it to `/etc/fstab` with `nofail`, and register it as a **Directory** storage with **VZDump backup file** content enabled. [6](#a1778fcc-dc77-41ea-adc9-de85aa9e800b)

### Step 5: Create Your First Virtual Machine

VMs provide full hardware isolation and can run any OS, including Windows.

1. **Upload an ISO:** Go to **local → ISO Images → Upload** (or Download from URL). Ubuntu Server 24.04 LTS is a common starting point. [2](#a1778fcc-dc77-41ea-adc9-de85aa9e800b)
2. **Create VM:** Click **Create VM** and configure:
   - **General:** Name and VM ID.
   - **OS:** Select your ISO.
   - **System:** Set **Machine** to `q35`, **BIOS** to `OVMF (UEFI)` for modern guests, and check **Add EFI Disk**. Enable **QEMU Agent** for clean shutdowns and IP reporting. [2](#a1778fcc-dc77-41ea-adc9-de85aa9e800b)
   - **Disks:** 32 GB+ using **VirtIO SCSI** controller. Enable **Discard** to allow TRIM to return freed blocks to the storage pool. [2](#a1778fcc-dc77-41ea-adc9-de85aa9e800b)
   - **CPU:** 2 cores, type `host` for best performance if you won’t live-migrate to different hardware. [2](#a1778fcc-dc77-41ea-adc9-de85aa9e800b)
   - **Memory:** 2048 MB. Enable **Ballooning** so unused RAM returns to the host. [2](#a1778fcc-dc77-41ea-adc9-de85aa9e800b)
   - **Network:** Attach to `vmbr0` with the **VirtIO** model. [2](#a1778fcc-dc77-41ea-adc9-de85aa9e800b)
3. Start the VM, open the **Console** tab, and install the OS. Once running, install the guest agent inside the VM:
   ```bash
   sudo apt install -y qemu-guest-agent
   sudo systemctl enable --now qemu-guest-agent
   ```
   The Proxmox UI will then display the VM’s IP address and support graceful shutdowns. [2](#a1778fcc-dc77-41ea-adc9-de85aa9e800b)

### Step 6: Create Your First LXC Container

LXC containers share the host kernel, start in seconds, and use far less RAM than VMs. They are ideal for Linux services like Pi-hole, Nextcloud, or Home Assistant. [6](#a1778fcc-dc77-41ea-adc9-de85aa9e800b)

1. **Download a template:** Go to **local → CT Templates → Templates** and download **Debian 12** or **Ubuntu 22.04**. [6](#a1778fcc-dc77-41ea-adc9-de85aa9e800b)
2. **Create CT:** Click **Create CT**:
   - Set hostname, root password, and optionally paste your SSH public key.
   - Choose the downloaded template.
   - Allocate disk (8 GB is enough for most services), 2 CPU cores, and 512 MB–2 GB RAM.
   - Set the network to DHCP or a static IP on `vmbr0`. [6](#a1778fcc-dc77-41ea-adc9-de85aa9e800b)
3. Finish and start. You can then `pct enter <id>` from the host shell or SSH directly into the container.

### Step 7: Configure Backups

Backups are the single feature that justifies moving off bare metal. [2](#a1778fcc-dc77-41ea-adc9-de85aa9e800b)

1. **Add backup storage:** Go to **Datacenter → Storage → Add** and choose **NFS**, **SMB/CIFS**, or a local **Directory**. Check **VZDump backup file** as the content type. [2](#a1778fcc-dc77-41ea-adc9-de85aa9e800b)
2. **Schedule backups:** Navigate to **Datacenter → Backup → Add**:
   - Select the target storage.
   - Choose a schedule (e.g., Saturday at 2 AM).
   - Set **Mode** to **Snapshot** for live backups with minimal downtime.
   - Enable compression (ZSTD is typical). [2](#a1778fcc-dc77-41ea-adc9-de85aa9e800b)

### Conclusion

You now have a fully functional Proxmox home server ready for self-hosting. Start with LXC containers for lightweight Linux services, use VMs when you need full isolation or non-Linux operating systems, and leverage ZFS snapshots and scheduled backups to protect your data. As your lab grows, you can explore PCI passthrough for GPUs, VLANs for network segmentation, and even clustering if you add a second node.

runs.questionText: Set up a Proxmox home server for self-hosting
runs.answerText:
## Setting Up a Proxmox Home Server for Self-Hosting

Proxmox VE is a free, Debian-based type-1 hypervisor that runs KVM virtual machines and LXC containers on the same host, managed through a web UI at port 8006. It is the foundation of most modern self-hosting stacks because it gives you snapshots, real backups, network segmentation, and isolation between services without per-host OS overhead. [1](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768) [8](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768)

### Hardware That Actually Works at Home

You do not need a rack server. The honest tiers for a home setup are:

- **Entry (5–10 light VMs/containers):** Mini PC with an Intel N100/N305 or Ryzen 5 8600G, 16–32 GB RAM, a single NVMe boot drive. Idle draw is 10–30 W. [1](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768) [9](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768)
- **Mid (15–30 VMs, one GPU passthrough):** Ryzen 9 9950X, 96 GB ECC UDIMM, ZFS boot mirror plus a power-loss-protected NVMe datastore. [9](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768)
- **Beast (multi-device passthrough):** Used EPYC 7313 on a Supermicro H12SSL-i with 128 GB RDIMM ECC. 128 PCIe Gen4 lanes means a GPU, HBA, and 10 GbE NIC each get their own clean IOMMU group. [9](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768)

Key things to spend money on: **RAM** (ZFS ARC and VMs both love it), a **second drive for the datastore** (so a dead disk does not kill the host), and an **Intel NIC** if the motherboard has a Realtek 2.5 GbE chip — Realtek NICs are a documented source of stalls under load. [1](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768) [9](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768)

Minimum for a learning box: x86-64 CPU with Intel VT-x or AMD-V enabled in firmware, 8 GB RAM, 120 GB SSD, wired Ethernet. [2](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768) [8](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768)

### Step 1: Download and Write the ISO

Grab the **Proxmox VE ISO Installer** from proxmox.com/en/downloads (around 1.8 GB) and verify the SHA256 sum against the value on the downloads page before flashing — a corrupted ISO produces baffling mid-install failures. [2](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768) [8](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768)

Write it to a USB drive (8 GB+):

- **Windows:** Rufus, choose GPT/UEFI and **DD Image mode** (not ISO mode). [2](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768) [8](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768)
- **macOS/Linux:** `dd` or Balena Etcher. With `dd`, double-check the device with `lsblk` first — picking the wrong target erases a real disk. [2](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768) [6](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768)

### Step 2: Install Proxmox VE

Boot from the USB (F11/F12/Esc for the one-time boot menu), choose **Install Proxmox VE (Graphical)**, and walk through:

1. **EULA** → accept.
2. **Target disk:** pick your NVMe/SSD. If you have two drives, the installer can lay down a **ZFS RAID-1 mirror** right here — say yes. [2](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768) [6](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768)
3. **Filesystem choice:**
   - `ext4` (LVM) — default, fine for a single drive.
   - `ZFS` — checksummed, snapshot-friendly, requires ~1 GB RAM per TB of pool for ARC.
   - `btrfs` is still "technology preview" — skip unless you know why you want it. [2](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768) [6](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768)
4. **Location, timezone, keyboard**, then a **strong root password** (12+ characters — this account owns the entire host).
5. **Network:** set a **static IP** on your LAN (e.g. `192.168.1.50/24`), gateway (`192.168.1.1`), DNS (`1.1.1.1`), and a hostname like `pve.home.lan`. [2](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768) [5](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768)
6. Click Install, wait 5–10 minutes, remove the USB, reboot. [2](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768)

### Step 3: First Web Login and Repository Cleanup

From another machine on the same network, open `https://192.168.1.50:8006`. Accept the self-signed certificate warning, log in as `root` with the **Linux PAM** realm. [2](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768) [6](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768)

The enterprise repo is enabled by default and needs a paid subscription to work. Switch to the free community repo via SSH or the in-browser Shell:

```bash
# Disable enterprise repo
sed -i 's/^deb/#deb/' /etc/apt/sources.list.d/pve-enterprise.list

# Add no-subscription repo
echo "deb http://download.proxmox.com/debian/pve bookworm pve-no-subscription" \
  > /etc/apt/sources.list.d/pve-no-subscription.list

# Update
apt update && apt full-upgrade -y
reboot
```

[2](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768) [5](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768) [6](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768)

You can also remove the "no valid subscription" nag popup by patching the web toolkit file and restarting `pveproxy`. The community tier is €110/year if you want to support the project later. [2](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768) [6](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768)

### Step 4: Storage Layout

The decision that bites people later is storage. Two rules cover most cases:

- **OS on one drive, VM data on another.** Single-disk layouts work right up until the disk dies, at which point you lose the host and every service at once. [1](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768)
- **Use a ZFS mirror for the boot pool** if you have two drives — a dead disk becomes a non-event (replace it, resilver, carry on). [1](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768) [2](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768)

The default install gives you two storage objects: `local` (ISOs, templates, backups) and `local-lvm` (VM disks). For a dedicated VM datastore, add the second drive as a Directory or ZFS pool in **Datacenter → Storage → Add**. [3](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768) [6](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768)

> ZFS protects against hardware failure, not against deleting the wrong thing. Off-host backups are still required. [1](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768)

### Step 5: Networking — Bridges and VLANs

`vmbr0` is the default Linux bridge that ties your physical NIC to every guest's virtual NIC — it acts as a virtual switch. [3](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768)

The upgrade that makes it powerful is **VLAN awareness**. Make the bridge VLAN-aware in the web UI, then drop each guest onto the right VLAN straight from its network settings. A managed switch carries the tagged VLANs to the Proxmox host over a trunk port. Public-facing services go in a DMZ segment that cannot reach your trusted LAN — enforced by default-deny firewall rules on the router. [1](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768) [3](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768)

### Step 6: Create Your First LXC Container

LXC containers share the host kernel, boot in under a second, and use a fraction of the RAM of a full VM. They are the right tool for trusted single-purpose Linux services. [1](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768) [6](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768)

1. Download a template in the UI: **local storage → CT Templates → Templates**, pick Debian or Ubuntu. [3](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768) [6](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768)
2. Click **Create CT**, give it a hostname, password, SSH key, 2 cores, 512 MB–2 GB RAM, 8–20 GB disk on `local-lvm`, and a static IP on `vmbr0`. [6](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768)

From the shell, a Debian 12 container takes one command:

```bash
pct create 101 local:vztmpl/debian-12-standard_12.0-1_amd64.tar.zst \
  --hostname grafana --cores 2 --memory 2048 --swap 512 \
  --net0 name=eth0,bridge=vmbr0,ip=192.168.1.101/24,gw=192.168.1.1 \
  --rootfs local-lvm:20 --password "..." \
  --ssh-public-keys ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub --start 1
pct enter 101
```

[6](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768)

### Step 7: Create Your First VM

Use a full VM when you need kernel isolation, a non-Linux OS, or to run Docker cleanly. Upload an ISO via **local → ISO Images → Upload**, then click **Create VM**. Sensible defaults for a Linux guest:

| Setting | Value | Why |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Machine | `q35` | Modern chipset, UEFI-ready |
| BIOS | `OVMF (UEFI)` + EFI disk | Modern boot path |
| Disk | 32 GB, `VirtIO SCSI`, **Discard** on | Trim returns blocks to the pool |
| CPU | 2 cores, type `host` | Best performance if you won't live-migrate |
| Memory | 2048 MB, **Ballooning** on | Returns unused RAM to the host |
| Network | `vmbr0`, `VirtIO` paravirtualized | 10× faster than e1000 emulation |
| QEMU Guest Agent | **enabled** | Clean shutdowns + IP reporting in UI |

[2](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768) [5](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768)

After installing the OS inside the VM:

```bash
sudo apt install -y qemu-guest-agent
sudo systemctl enable --now qemu-guest-agent
```

The VM's IP will then appear on the Proxmox Summary page. [2](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768)

> A reliable rule of thumb: **one well-resourced VM for the whole Docker stack**, separate LXC containers for standalone Linux services. Running Docker inside LXC is possible but adds permission debugging that a clean VM avoids. [1](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768)

### Step 8: Backups and Snapshots

This is the single feature that justifies moving off bare metal. [2](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768)

- **Snapshot** — instant "freeze" of a VM/container. Free, fast, ideal before risky changes. Not a backup. [3](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768)
- **VZDump backup** — full, compressed, scheduled. Add a storage target first (**Datacenter → Storage → Add → NFS/PBS/Directory**), then schedule a job (**Datacenter → Backup → Add**). Use Snapshot mode with ZSTD compression for minimal downtime. [2](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768) [3](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768)

**The non-negotiable rule: backups go to a different physical machine.** Backups on the same disk survive nothing. Add an off-site copy for fire/theft. [1](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768) [2](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768)

Boring routine that works: snapshot before every update, nightly backups of important guests to a NAS, periodic off-site copy. [1](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768)

### Step 9: Remote Access Without Exposing Yourself

Never port-forward the Proxmox web UI (port 8006) to the internet. A hypervisor management plane exposed publicly is exactly the target you do not want to be. [1](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768) [7](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768)

The right pattern is a VPN into your own network — WireGuard on the firewall — so the management plane stays private and you reach it as if you were home. Tailscale is the friction-free alternative if you do not want to manage a VPN server. [1](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768) [4](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768)

For services that do need to be reachable from the internet, put a **reverse proxy** (Nginx Proxy Manager or Traefik) in front of them with automatic HTTPS via Let's Encrypt — usually combined with Cloudflare Tunnel so you do not open a single inbound port. [1](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768) [5](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768)

### What to Build on Top

Once the platform is in place, the typical build order is:

1. **One Docker VM** (Ubuntu Server, 4–8 GB RAM) — install Docker and Portainer. This is where most of your apps live.
2. **Reverse proxy LXC or container** — exposes services under clean hostnames with HTTPS.
3. **Per-service LXCs** — Pi-hole/AdGuard, Home Assistant, Gitea, Uptime Kuma, etc. [1](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768) [5](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768)

A typical real-world stack on a single mini PC: 1 Docker VM, 4–6 service LXCs, 1 NAS VM (if storage outgrows the host), 1 monitoring LXC. Keep a text file describing every guest, its purpose, VLAN, and backup status so the host stays legible six months later. [1](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768) [5](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768)

### Mistakes That Hurt Later

- Installing on a single SSD with no redundancy. [1](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768)
- Leaving the bridge VLAN-unaware. [1](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768)
- Storing backups on the same machine as the VMs. [1](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768) [2](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768)
- Over-provisioning RAM to guests so the host starves. [1](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768)
- Spinning up a VM for every idea until the host juggles fifteen half-used guests. [1](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768)
- Port-forwarding port 8006 to the internet. [1](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768) [7](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768)
- Moving every critical household service onto the box on day one, before backups and restore are tested end-to-end. [7](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768)

### Pre-Flight Checklist

Before declaring the host production-ready, prove the loop on a disposable VM: install the guest agent, shut it down cleanly from Proxmox and from inside the guest, take a backup, delete or stop the original, restore the backup under a new VM ID, confirm it boots with the expected disk, network, and application state, and record the actual backup size, duration, and restore duration. Only then move real services in. [7](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768)

Build the platform once, properly, and every service you add afterwards is a ten-minute job instead of a weekend. [1](#6fa72d86-d1a4-47eb-ad0e-12c29de55768)
